common_name: Bird's Beak Oncidium (Bird's Mouth Orchid, Dove Orchid, Pink Oncidium)

planting.note: Grow in a small pot with fine to medium bark or in a slatted basket in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun; Oncidium ornithorhynchum blooms in fall and winter on long, arching, much-branched racemes that can reach 24 inches and carry 100–200 small flowers in rose-pink to lilac-lavender — the entire inflorescence is extraordinarily floriferous and the fragrance (a sweet, vanilla-baby-powder scent) is among the most pleasant of any orchid. A cool-to-intermediate grower that appreciates nighttime temperatures of 55–62°F in fall to trigger blooming. Protect from frost. One of the best fall-blooming orchids for zone 9-11 outdoor culture. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning light in fine to medium bark or on a mount — Bird's Beak Oncidium grows most vigorously in intermediate conditions and produces its most spectacular, multi-branched bloom spikes when given cool, dry fall nights that trigger the inflorescence development. Oncidium ornithorhynchum is a compact, sympodial, epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to Mexico, Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Costa Rica, and Colombia — one of the most free-flowering and fragrant of the many smaller Oncidium species, widely grown by orchid enthusiasts for the extraordinary fall flower display and remarkable fragrance. The plant is small and compact — oval, slightly flattened pseudobulbs 2–3 inches tall with 2–3 soft, narrow leaves. In fall and early winter, multiple flower spikes emerge from the base of the pseudobulbs and branch repeatedly to produce long, arching, pendant racemes bearing 50–200 small (0.5-inch) flowers in rose-pink to lilac-lavender, with a small, distinctly shaped lip that gives the species name ornithorhynchum (Greek: 'bird's beak'). The sheer flower quantity on a well-grown, established plant creates a cascade of soft pink-lavender that is exceptional among small orchids. The fragrance is the plant's other outstanding quality: intensely sweet, warm, and pleasant — commonly described as resembling vanilla, baby powder, or sweet clover — produced throughout the day and most concentrated in warm morning conditions. An outstanding fall-blooming orchid for zone 9-12. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Oncidium ornithorhynchum cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Oncidium ornithorhynchum species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Cool-growing Oncidium species for zone 9-11
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Oncidium (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Spider Orchid (Arching Brassia, Colombian Spider Orchid, Arcuigera Brassia)

planting.note: Grow in a medium-to-large pot with medium bark or in a slatted basket in bright indirect to partial direct light; Brassia arcuigera is one of the largest-flowered Brassia species, with individual spidery flowers reaching 6–10 inches tip to tip of the longest extended sepals. The flowers are vivid yellow-green to yellow with brown to cinnamon spotting and blotching on the sepals and petals, with a light, pleasant fragrance. Blooms primarily in spring. The spidery flowers mimic female spider wasps, and male wasps attempt to mate with the lip, effecting pollination. Intermediate temperatures preferred. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning light in medium bark — Spider Orchid grows most vigorously in intermediate temperatures with bright, filtered light and produces its extraordinary long-sepaled flowers most freely when given generous pot size and adequate root room. Brassia arcuigera is a robust, sympodial, epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru — one of the larger-flowered Brassia species (a genus sometimes called 'spider orchids' for the elongated, spreading, insect-leg-like sepals of their flowers). The plant produces flattened, compressed pseudobulbs 4–5 inches tall with 2–3 broad, somewhat fleshy, strap-like leaves. In spring, long (to 24 inches), arching flower spikes emerge bearing 6–10 large flowers, each one remarkable for the extraordinary length of the sepals: the lower lateral sepals extend downward and outward in a strongly arching pattern (giving the species name arcuigera, 'bearing an arch') to total flower widths of 6–10 inches tip-to-tip. The flower color is vivid yellow-green to bright yellow with irregular brown to cinnamon spotting concentrated toward the sepal bases, and the broad lip is white with brown spots. The spidery appearance of the flower is an adaptation to its pollination system — male spider wasps (Pepsis species) mistake the lip for a large female wasp and attempt to mate with it, picking up pollinaria in the process. A spectacular orchid of extraordinary flower form. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Brassia arcuigera cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Brassia arcuigera species profile
Dodson, C.H. — Pollination of Brassia by spider wasps (Orchid Digest)
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Brassia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Blue Fox Tail Orchid (Sky-Blue Rhynchostylis, Celestial Orchid, Blue-Lipped Rhynchostylis)

planting.note: Grow in a slatted basket with coarse bark or bare-rooted in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun with good air circulation; Rhynchostylis coelestis is a small, compact vandaceous orchid from Thailand and Vietnam that produces erect to arching, dense cylindrical racemes of small (0.5-inch) white flowers heavily spotted and the lip colored in vivid sky-blue to violet-blue — making it one of the very few orchids with true blue coloring. Blooms summer to fall. More compact and easier to manage than the larger R. gigantea. Intermediate to warm growing. Good air circulation is important. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun in a slatted basket with good air circulation — Blue Fox Tail Orchid grows most vigorously in bright, well-ventilated, warm conditions and produces its distinctive blue-tinged flower spikes most freely in summer to fall with appropriate light and temperature. Rhynchostylis coelestis is a compact, monopodial, epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to Thailand and Vietnam, a member of the small genus Rhynchostylis in the tribe Vandeae — closely related to Vanda and Aerides and sharing their strap-leaved, monopodial growth habit and preference for bright light and good air circulation. The plant is compact — rarely exceeding 10–18 inches in height — with leathery, strap-shaped, dark-green leaves in two ranks. In summer and fall, erect to arching, dense, cylindrical racemes 6–10 inches long emerge from the leaf axils, packed with small (0.5-inch) flowers that create the plant's exceptional ornamental quality: the flowers are white to pale blue-tinted at the petal tips, but the lip (the enlarged front petal) is a vivid, saturated sky-blue to violet-blue — a true blue flower color unusual in orchids and in plants generally. The blue lip contrasts dramatically with the white to spotted white flower body, creating a jewel-box quality in the dense, rounded raceme. Rhynchostylis coelestis has contributed its blue color to many hybrid Vanda and vandaceous orchids. The species epithet coelestis means 'sky-colored' in Latin, directly referencing the blue flowers. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Rhynchostylis coelestis cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Rhynchostylis coelestis species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Rhynchostylis: compact vandaceous orchids
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Rhynchostylis (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Chinese Cymbidium (Sin Mui Orchid, Chinese New Year Orchid, Dark Cymbidium)

planting.note: Grow in a medium pot with fine to medium bark or peat-based orchid mix in partial to full shade with cool fall temperatures; Cymbidium sinense is the most important and widely cultivated of the 'Chinese' or 'Oriental' cymbidiums — small-flowered, highly fragrant species that have been cultivated in China and Japan for over 2,000 years for their elegant, thin-petaled flowers and intense, elusive fragrance. Unlike the large-flowered hybrid cymbidiums, C. sinense produces smaller (1.5-inch), darker flowers in maroon, red-brown, or greenish-brown with a white lip. Cool fall nights (50–58°F) are essential to trigger blooming. Hardy to zone 8 with protection. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in a medium pot with fine to medium bark or humus-rich terrestrial orchid mix — Chinese Cymbidium grows most successfully when given cool fall temperatures (50–58°F nights) to trigger blooming and tolerates considerably more shade than the large-flowered hybrid cymbidiums used in the cut flower trade. Cymbidium sinense is a terrestrial to semi-epiphytic, sympodial orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to southern China, Hong Kong, Taiwan, Japan (Ryukyu Islands), Vietnam, and India (Assam) — the most significant species in the group of 'Oriental' or 'Chinese' cymbidiums that have been cultivated in China and Japan for more than 2,000 years, centuries before the development of the large-flowered hybrids that now dominate Western horticulture. The Chinese, Japanese, and Korean horticultural traditions around C. sinense and its relatives (the 'koran' or Chinese orchid culture) emphasize the subtle fragrance, elegant simplicity, and venerable cultural associations of these species over the bold colors of hybrid cymbidiums. The plant produces strap-like, dark-green, arching leaves 24–30 inches long and, in winter, erect flower spikes bearing 10–20 small (1.5-inch) flowers in maroon, red-brown to dark wine-red, or greenish-brown with a white, red-spotted lip. The fragrance is the defining quality: elusive, refined, and distinct — described as 'subtle orchid fragrance' or a soft, spicy-sweet floral scent, most perceptible close-up in cool morning conditions. The 'Chinese New Year' connection references the winter to early spring bloom timing. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Cymbidium sinense: Chinese Cymbidium cultivation
Royal Horticultural Society — Oriental Cymbidium species: culture and history
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Cymbidium sinense species profile
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Cymbidium (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Variable Maxillaria (Yellow and Red Maxillaria, Tufted Maxillaria, Variabilis Orchid)

planting.note: Grow mounted on bark or in a small basket with coarse bark in bright indirect to partial direct light; Maxillaria variabilis is one of the most tolerant and adaptable of the Maxillaria species, growing in a wide range of temperature and light conditions — cool, intermediate, and warm — and producing single, small (0.75-inch) flowers in variable colors (yellow, orange-red, red, or brown) singly from between the pseudobulbs and leaf bases on short stems. The plant forms increasingly large clumps over time, eventually producing dozens of flowers simultaneously over many months. A good beginner orchid for zone 9-11 outdoor culture. Tolerates brief temperatures to 35°F. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning light mounted on bark or in a small basket — Variable Maxillaria is one of the most adaptable and undemanding of the smaller Maxillaria species, tolerating cool to warm temperatures, a range of light conditions, and irregular watering better than many more exacting orchids. Maxillaria variabilis is a sympodial, clustering, epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, Panama, and Colombia — widely distributed in the cloud forests and humid montane forests of Central America and northern South America. The plant produces compact, somewhat flattened pseudobulbs 1–2 inches tall arranged tightly on a clustering rhizome, each topped with a single, narrow, grass-like leaf 4–8 inches long. The species name variabilis refers to the remarkable variation in flower color across the natural range: from clear, bright yellow in some populations to orange-red, brick-red, deep red, or brown-red in others, with all intermediate shades represented. The flowers are small (0.75 inch), produced singly on short stems from the bases of the pseudobulbs, and are faintly fragrant. The real value of the plant lies in its clumping habit: over several years, a plant develops into a large, multi-headed clump producing dozens of flowers simultaneously over the extended blooming season (spring and early summer primarily, with scattered bloom at other times). Very adaptable and rewarding in zone 9-11. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Maxillaria variabilis cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Maxillaria variabilis species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Maxillaria species for Florida outdoor orchid culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Maxillaria (non-toxic to pets)
